explanation_md: The semicolon immediately after `if (x > 0)` is a null statement — it is the entire body of the `if`. The `cout` line is a separate statement that always runs regardless of the condition.

options.b: `n = 10` is an assignment, not a comparison — it sets `n` to 10 and the condition is always true
options.c: The `else` branch is unreachable and causes a compile error

explanation_md: `n = 10` assigns 10 to `n` and evaluates to 10, which is non-zero (true). The condition is always true regardless of `n`'s original value. The fix is `n == 10`.
`4 % 2` is 0, so `isOdd(4)` returns false. `false == false` is true, so the `then` branch runs and prints `Even`.

prompt_md: Why should you avoid writing `if (condition == true)` in C++?

options.a: It causes a compile error because `bool` cannot be compared with `==`
options.b: It adds no information and a typo (`== false`) silently inverts the logic
options.c: It makes the program run slower at runtime
options.d: It is only valid inside function templates

explanation_md: `if (condition)` and `if (condition == true)` behave identically. The `== true` form is redundant, and if a programmer accidentally writes `== false` instead, the entire condition is silently negated — a logic bug with no compile error.

prompt_md: Which compiler flag helps detect the accidental null statement `if (x > 0);`?
explanation_md: `-Wall` (and `-Wextra`) enables extra diagnostics including warnings about empty bodies after `if` and assignment used as a truth value. `-std=c++20` sets the language standard; `-O2` controls optimization; `-fno-exceptions` disables exception handling.
